title: The Week That Was
gist: A weekly Lawfare roundup whose substantive signal is concentrated in AI governance and national-security tech: Trump executive orders on quantum computing and post-quantum cryptography, a DOD intervention in the NAACP suit against xAI, litigation over California's AI training-data transparency law, and the export-control order on Anthropic's top models.

insights.claim: Trump signed executive orders on quantum computing directing federal agencies to implement stronger protections against quantum-enabled cryptographic attacks and to position the U.S. as world leader in quantum computing, including a federal effort to build a large quantum computer.
insights.speaker: Sarah Willrich
insights.so_what: Federal push into post-quantum cryptography and quantum hardware is a policy catalyst for the quantum and cybersecurity sectors; watch agency procurement.
insights.quote: direct federal agencies to implement stronger protections against quantum computing-enabled cryptographic attacks
insights.locator: Sarah Willrich shared Trump's executive orders on quantum computing

insights.claim: The Department of Defense intervened in the NAACP's lawsuit against xAI, and a separate lawsuit challenges the Trump administration's export-control order on Anthropic's top AI models.
insights.speaker: Sarah Willrich
insights.so_what: AI firms face active litigation and export-control entanglement with the government; xAI and Anthropic are specific legal-risk names to track.
insights.quote: the Department of Defense's intervention in the NAACP's lawsuit against xAI

insights.claim: Amid xAI's First Amendment challenge to California's AI training-data transparency law (Assembly Bill No. 2013), analysts argued such disclosure requirements could constitute 'compelled commercial speech' with potential legal consequences.
insights.speaker: Sarah Willrich
insights.so_what: AI training-data transparency mandates may be vulnerable on First Amendment grounds—a regulatory uncertainty bearing on state-level AI disclosure laws.
insights.quote: could constitute “compelled commercial speech” and the potential legal consequences thereof
insights.locator: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj and Mackenzie Arnold

insights.claim: Jessica Tillipman argued the frontier-model evaluation framework in Trump's AI executive order is nominally voluntary but becomes de facto regulation because of the government's outsized power as a buyer of the models.
insights.speaker: Sarah Willrich
insights.so_what: Reinforces that AI-governance compulsion will flow through procurement, not licensing—the binding channel for model developers.
insights.quote: nominally voluntary but becomes de facto regulation due to the government's outsized power as a buyer of the models

insights.claim: Anthropic shut down its Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models, per the Seriously Risky Business newsletter recap.
insights.speaker: Sarah Willrich
insights.so_what: Model-lifecycle event at a frontier lab; relevant to tracking the open-weight/Mythos debate referenced across the week's coverage.
insights.quote: Anthropic's shutdown of its Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models
insights.locator: In last week's Seriously Risky Business cybersecurity newsletter

insights.claim: Seth Stodder warned that cheap mass-produced drones have transformed warfare in ways that threaten U.S. cities and critical infrastructure while U.S. counter-drone defenses are nearly nonexistent, and Philip Rohlfing flagged that new counter-UAS authority for state and local law enforcement is bottlenecked by a single federal certification schoolhouse.
insights.speaker: Sarah Willrich
insights.so_what: Converging coverage on the drone threat and a certification bottleneck points to an urgent, under-supplied counter-UAS market.
insights.quote: cheap, mass-produced drones have transformed warfare in ways that pose a serious and underappreciated threat to U.S. cities

insights.claim: Kenneth Propp analyzed the EU's proposed Cloud and AI Development Act (CADA) as a bid for tech sovereignty whose higher assurance levels put U.S. companies in direct conflict with U.S. law, reflecting transatlantic estrangement accelerated by Trump-era sanctions.
insights.speaker: Sarah Willrich
insights.so_what: EU CADA is a structural threat to U.S. cloud incumbents' European market share—a transatlantic regulatory divergence to track.
insights.quote: CADA's higher assurance levels put U.S. companies in direct conflict with U.S. law
